items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

Students lump all training together. Plyometrics specifically targets POWER - explosive jumps and bounds use rapid eccentric-then-concentric muscle action, training the stretch-shortening cycle for fast force production.

items.q: What is the medical name for dangerously HIGH body temperature?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hyperthermia. “Hypothermia” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which therapy uses cold to reduce swelling?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cryotherapy. “Hydrotherapy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many phases does rehabilitation typically have?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
